requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must strategically convert pre-tax traditional IRA, SEP-IRA, or SIMPLE IRA balances to Roth IRA paying taxes currently to achieve tax-free growth and distributions, optimally timed during low-income years or market downturns
criteria: Have existing traditional IRA, SEP-IRA, or SIMPLE IRA with pre-tax contributions and earnings
No income limits on Roth conversions (unlike direct Roth contributions)
Can convert any amount; entire balance or partial conversions allowed
Conversion creates taxable income in year of conversion at ordinary income rates
Strategic timing: convert during low-income years, early retirement, market downturns, or before RMDs begin
Consider bracket management: convert up to top of target bracket to minimize marginal rate
Must wait 2 years after SIMPLE IRA contribution before converting to avoid 25% penalty
Multi-year conversion strategy can spread tax liability across multiple years optimizing total tax paid

roth_conversion_strategy.no_income_limits: Unlike contributions, anyone can convert regardless of income level
roth_conversion_strategy.unlimited_amounts: Can convert any amount from traditional IRA to Roth IRA
roth_conversion_strategy.tax_payment_options: Pay conversion tax from outside sources to maximize Roth balance growth
roth_conversion_strategy.bracket_management: Convert up to top of 12%, 22%, or 24% bracket to manage marginal rate
roth_conversion_strategy.low_income_opportunity: Early retirement, business loss years, or sabbatical years ideal for conversions
roth_conversion_strategy.market_timing: Convert when market down; pay tax on lower value then enjoy tax-free recovery
roth_conversion_strategy.five_year_rule: Each conversion has separate 5-year clock for penalty-free principal withdrawal

restrictions: Conversion creates ordinary income potentially pushing into higher tax brackets and affecting other tax items
Increased AGI affects Medicare premiums (IRMAA), net investment income tax, QBI deduction, and other income-based provisions
Cannot recharacterize Roth conversions after 2017 TCJA; conversion is permanent decision
Must pay taxes on conversion; using IRA funds to pay taxes reduces amount converted and future growth
5-year rule: converted amounts subject to 10% penalty if withdrawn within 5 years before age 59½
Each conversion has separate 5-year period creating tracking complexity for multiple conversions
State tax implications: conversion taxable in conversion year; some states have different Roth treatment
Required minimum distributions (RMDs) cannot be converted; must take RMD first then convert other amounts
